directions.name: Restore The Best Measured PTX Grouping Window On The Accepted Surface
directions.hypothesis: The latest measured run landed at 30.429696 ms, still +6.265424 ms away from the accepted 24.164272 ms anchor. The fastest way to keep the loop grounded is to replay the exact accepted PTX surface before reopening another branch.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Search drift away from the accepted PTX steady state rather than a missing structural opportunity.
directions.risk: Low. This is the known accepted PTX anchor surface.
ranking_notes: Use restore whenever the active branch drifts or the prior variant closed as a loss.
This keeps the loop anchored to the best-known PTX surface before the next replay.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if this replay does not beat the active anchor cleanly or if it broadens beyond an exact bounded historical surface.

directions.name: Port Grouped-Row Traversal Into The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
directions.hypothesis: The latest run is 30.429696 ms against an accepted anchor of 24.164272 ms. Round round 59/100 should replay the bounded `non_ptx_grouped` family once from the current state so the search keeps advancing instead of restoring forever.
directions.expected_bottleneck: A branch-local hot-band scheduling or geometry bottleneck on the dominant kernel path, rather than a pure restore-only action.
directions.risk: Moderate. This branch swaps the dominant hot-band dispatch away from the PTX winner, so it can regress meaningfully if the sibling surface is still under-tuned.
ranking_notes: Replay the `non_ptx_grouped` family as an exact historical surface to keep the loop bounded and auditable.
Keep restore as the deterministic fallback behind any replay branch.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if this replay does not beat the active anchor cleanly or if it broadens beyond an exact bounded historical surface.
